items.title: Layered architecture in ASP.NET Core MVC
items.description: Organize the system into concentric layers — presentation (controllers/views), application/domain services, and data access — where dependencies point inward toward stable abstractions and never outward toward the web framework. In .NET this is typically expressed as separate projects (Core, Data, Rag, Agent, Web) so the domain has no compile-time reference to ASP.NET Core or EF Core internals. This enforces separation of concerns, makes the domain testable in isolation, and prevents accidental cycles that erode maintainability.
items.applicability: Applies to any non-trivial ASP.NET Core MVC solution that is expected to evolve, especially multi-project solutions where domain logic must outlive UI or persistence technology choices.
items.example: LocalAIFactory splits Core (entities, abstractions), Data (EF Core AppDbContext, migrations), and Web (controllers, DI root) into distinct projects with no dependency cycles; Core references nothing.
items.limitation: Over-layering a small app adds ceremony with little payoff; do not create a service or repository indirection that only forwards calls with no behavior.

items.title: Controller to Service to Repository flow
items.description: Controllers translate HTTP into intent and back (model binding, validation, status codes) but contain no business logic; they delegate to application services that orchestrate domain rules and transactions; services in turn use repositories (or the DbContext) for persistence. Keeping each tier thin and single-purpose means business decisions live in one testable place and the same operation can be reused by an API, a background job, or a CLI without duplicating logic.
items.applicability: Use for any request that does more than a trivial read-and-render, particularly write operations and workflows that span multiple entities or external calls.
items.example: A POST handler calls _projectService.ImportAsync(dto, ct); the service validates rules and enqueues work, while the controller only returns RedirectToAction or a 4xx on failure.
items.limitation: For simple read-only list/detail pages, an extra service layer can be pure overhead — a controller querying a lightweight read model directly is acceptable.

items.title: Dependency injection lifetimes (singleton, scoped, transient)
items.description: ASP.NET Core's built-in container offers three lifetimes: singleton (one instance for the app), scoped (one per request/scope), and transient (a new instance per resolution). The defining hazard is the captive dependency: resolving a scoped service (notably EF Core's DbContext) from a singleton captures it for the app's lifetime, causing cross-request state leakage and concurrency corruption. Choose scoped for anything tied to a unit of work, singleton for stateless caches/clients, and transient for cheap, stateless helpers.
items.applicability: Applies whenever registering services in Program.cs / DI configuration, and especially when a long-lived component (singleton, hosted service) needs a short-lived one.
items.example: A HealthMonitorService hosted singleton must not inject AppDbContext directly; it creates a scope via IServiceScopeFactory.CreateScope() per probe and resolves the scoped DbContext inside it.
items.limitation: DbContext is not thread-safe; never share a scoped DbContext across parallel tasks — give each task its own scope instead of widening the lifetime.

items.title: Entity Framework Core change tracking and AsNoTracking
items.description: EF Core's change tracker snapshots every entity returned by a tracking query so it can compute UPDATE/DELETE statements on SaveChanges. For read-only queries this snapshotting costs CPU and memory and can produce subtle identity-resolution surprises; AsNoTracking() skips it, returning lighter, detached objects. Use tracking only when you intend to mutate and persist the loaded entities within the same context.
items.applicability: Use AsNoTracking for list/grid/report and any read-only projection; keep tracking enabled for load-modify-save workflows where the loaded graph will be updated.
items.example: var rows = await _db.Projects.AsNoTracking().Select(p => new ProjectRow(p.Id, p.Name)).ToListAsync(ct); avoids tracking overhead on a dashboard list.
items.limitation: AsNoTracking entities are detached, so later SaveChanges will not persist changes to them unless explicitly re-attached; do not use it on the read step of an update.

items.title: EF Core migrations: additive and backward-compatible
items.description: Prefer migrations that only add tables, columns, and indexes, with new columns nullable or given safe defaults, so old and new application versions can coexist against the same schema during rolling deploys. Destructive changes (dropping/renaming columns, lossy type changes, deleting data) break running instances and are hard to roll back, so they should be staged: add new, backfill, switch reads, and only later remove the old shape under explicit approval. Keep the generated ModelSnapshot consistent by always going through the EF tooling rather than hand-editing.
items.applicability: Applies to every schema change in an MSSQL + EF Core system, and is mandatory wherever zero-downtime or backward compatibility across versions is required.
items.example: Adding ImportedFile.DetectedEncoding as a nullable string is additive; renaming it later would require an expand/contract sequence with a backfill step.
items.limitation: The expand/contract pattern means temporarily carrying redundant columns and extra deploys; for greenfield or pre-release schemas a direct destructive migration may be simpler and acceptable.

items.title: Async/await and CancellationToken discipline
items.description: Use async I/O end-to-end so request threads are released back to the pool while waiting on the database, network, or disk, which is what lets ASP.NET Core scale under load. Flow a CancellationToken from the controller action through services to the EF Core and HttpClient calls so that aborted requests and shutdown promptly release resources instead of running orphaned work. Avoid sync-over-async (.Result/.Wait()), which can deadlock and starves the thread pool, and prefer ConfigureAwait(false) in library code.
items.applicability: Applies to every method that performs I/O on the request path or in hosted services; the token should originate from HttpContext.RequestAborted or the host's stopping token.
items.example: public async Task<IActionResult> Index(CancellationToken ct) => View(await _svc.GetAsync(ct)); the ct reaches _db.ToListAsync(ct).
items.limitation: Making CPU-bound or trivially synchronous code async adds state-machine overhead and obscures stack traces without benefit; reserve async for genuine I/O.

items.title: Structured logging
items.description: Log with message templates and named parameters (e.g. logger.LogInformation("Imported {FileCount} files for project {ProjectId}", count, id)) rather than pre-interpolated strings, so each log event carries queryable, typed properties. Structured events let you filter and aggregate by field in a log store, correlate work via scopes/correlation IDs, and keep noise controlled by level. This turns logs from prose into data, which is the difference between grepping and actually diagnosing production incidents.
items.applicability: Applies to all server-side logging via Microsoft.Extensions.Logging; especially valuable for request pipelines, background jobs, and long-running imports.
items.example: RequestTimingMiddleware logs '<- {path} {status} in {ms} ms' so a started line with no matching completed line pinpoints a hung endpoint.
items.limitation: Do not log secrets, PII, or large payloads (full file contents); high-cardinality fields can bloat indexes and cost. Use Debug/Trace for verbose detail to avoid drowning Information.

items.title: Background jobs via IHostedService and queues
items.description: Offload slow or bursty work (imports, embeddings, health probes) from the request path to a BackgroundService that drains an in-memory or durable queue, so HTTP requests stay fast and the work runs under a single controlled worker. The hosted service must create its own DI scope per work item to obtain scoped dependencies like DbContext, honor the host's stopping token for graceful shutdown, and isolate failures so one bad item does not kill the loop. This decouples user-perceived latency from processing time and smooths load spikes.
items.applicability: Use when an operation is too slow to block a request, must be serialized, or should survive the originating request — e.g. ZIP import pipelines and periodic background probes.
items.example: IngestionBackgroundService dequeues import jobs and processes each in its own scope; HealthMonitorService probes Qdrant/Ollama on a timer and caches the result for controllers to read.
items.limitation: An in-memory queue loses pending work on restart and does not span multiple instances; for durability or scale-out, back it with a persistent queue or store and make handlers idempotent.

items.title: Options and configuration pattern
items.description: Bind configuration sections to strongly-typed POCO option classes and inject IOptions<T> (or IOptionsSnapshot/IOptionsMonitor for reloadable values) instead of reading magic strings from IConfiguration throughout the codebase. This gives compile-time structure, a single place to validate settings at startup with ValidateOnStart, and clean testability by constructing options directly. Feature flags (e.g. whether Qdrant/Ollama are enabled) become explicit, typed switches rather than scattered string lookups.
items.applicability: Use for any cohesive group of settings — connection details, feature toggles, model names, timeouts — bound from appsettings.json and environment variables.
items.limitation: IOptions<T> is a singleton and will not reflect config reloads; use IOptionsSnapshot in scoped contexts when live reload matters, and never embed secrets in committed config files.

items.title: Server-side validation patterns
items.description: Always validate on the server regardless of any client-side checks, because client validation is a UX convenience that an attacker or a non-browser client can bypass entirely. Combine model-level validation (data annotations / ModelState, or FluentValidation) for shape and format with domain invariants enforced in services for business rules that depend on current state. Return clear, field-level errors for input problems (400/422) and treat validation as a security boundary, not just a usability nicety.
items.applicability: Applies to every endpoint that accepts external input — form posts, API payloads, query parameters, and uploaded files — in any trust context.
items.example: if (!ModelState.IsValid) return View(model); plus a service-level guard that rejects an import for a project the caller has no access to, preventing IDOR.
items.limitation: Annotations cover stateless shape only; cross-entity and authorization rules must live in the service/domain layer. Over-strict validation can reject legitimate edge cases, so validate intent, not incidental formatting.

items.title: Centralized error handling
items.description: Handle unexpected exceptions in one place — exception-handling middleware or UseExceptionHandler — rather than wrapping every action in try/catch, so failures produce consistent responses, are logged uniformly, and never leak stack traces or internals to clients. Distinguish expected conditions (not found, validation, forbidden) returning specific status codes from genuinely unexpected faults returning 500 with a correlation ID the user can quote to support. A standard problem-details body keeps API errors machine-readable and consistent.
items.applicability: Applies at the application boundary of every web app and API; pair with per-operation catch only where you can meaningfully recover or add context.
items.example: app.UseExceptionHandler() in production renders a generic error page and logs the exception with a correlation ID, while a NotFoundException maps to a 404 via the same pipeline.
items.limitation: Centralized handling should not swallow exceptions that indicate corrupted state — let those crash fast; and avoid catch-all blocks that hide bugs or convert programming errors into silent no-ops.

items.title: Repository vs service responsibilities
items.description: A repository owns persistence concerns — querying, materializing, and saving aggregates — and exposes a collection-like abstraction over the data store; a service owns business orchestration, invariants, transaction boundaries, and coordination across multiple repositories or external systems. Keeping them distinct prevents business rules from leaking into queries and SQL details from leaking into domain logic. In EF Core the DbContext is already a Unit of Work plus repositories, so an extra repository layer is justified mainly for testability, query reuse, or insulating the domain from EF.
items.applicability: Apply when business logic spans multiple entities or external calls, or when you need to mock data access in tests; the boundary is most valuable in larger, longer-lived systems.
items.example: ProjectService.ImportAsync coordinates validation, calls IFileRepository to persist files, and queues embedding work; the repository only knows how to read/write ImportedFile rows.
items.limitation: Wrapping EF Core in thin pass-through repositories that just forward LINQ adds layers without value and can hide EF's rich querying; do not abstract for its own sake.

items.title: Unit vs integration testing boundaries
items.description: Unit tests verify a single component's logic in isolation with dependencies substituted, running fast and deterministically to pin business rules; integration tests exercise real collaborators — the EF Core provider, the database, middleware — to catch wiring, mapping, and query-translation defects that mocks hide. A healthy suite is mostly fast unit tests with a focused set of integration tests over the riskiest seams (persistence, DI, HTTP pipeline). Test behavior and contracts, not private implementation detail, so refactoring does not break the suite.
items.applicability: Use unit tests for domain/service logic and edge cases; use integration tests for repository queries, migrations, authorization, and end-to-end request handling via WebApplicationFactory.
items.example: Mock IFileRepository to unit-test import rules; run an integration test against a real (or LocalDB/SQL Express) database to confirm a LINQ query actually translates and returns the expected rows.
items.limitation: In-memory EF providers do not faithfully emulate MSSQL behavior (e.g. SQL translation, transactions), so they give false confidence — integration tests should target a real SQL Server-compatible engine; integration suites are slower and need careful data setup/teardown.
